content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 8,
1994
3. Scott Zundel
City Attorney
69-825 Highway 111
Rancho Mirage,
CA 92270
Re:
Your Request For Advice
Our File No.
1—94—111
Dear Mr.
Zundel:
We respond to your March 24,
1994, letter requesting
Commission advice regarding your local ordinance on campaign
reform.
As your letter does not request advice with regard to a
specific set of facts,
we have treated your request as one for
informal advice.
1
Under the Political Reform Act (the “Act”)
2
, the Commission
is empowered to give advice with respect to the provisions of the
Act.
(Section 83114.)
While the interpretation of local
ordinances regulating campaign finance are generally beyond the
scope of Commission advice (g Kunkel Advice Letter,
No.
1—89—598),
the Act does contain several provisions regarding
the implementation of such ordinances.
(Sections 81009.5,
81013
and 85100.)
The Commission’s advice must necessarily be limited
to the application of those sections.
(Gawron Advice Letter,
No.
1—89—550.)
We caution that the Commission cannot provide advice on the
operation of your local ordinance.
Ketner Advice Letter,
No.
1-93—464.)
Primary responsibility, for interpreting the ordinance
will fall on you, the city attorney.
(Kunkel Advice Letter,
supra.)
Moreover, the Commission cannot advise you on
1
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329 Cc) (3).)
2
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Codeof Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
1—94—111
page
2
constitutional issues which have impacted campaign reform in other
arenas and may be applicable to your ordinance.
Generally,
where the provisions of a local law conflict with
the Act, the local law is superseded.
(Section 81013.)
However,
the Act does not prevent a local government from imposing lower
campaign contribution limits for candidates for elective office in
its own jurisdiction.
(Section 85101.)
Accordingly, the Act does
not prevent local jurisdictions from either imposing lower
contribution limits or from imposing other political campaign
requirements so long as the requirements do not prevent candidates
from complying with the Act.
(Van Winkle Advice Letter,
No.
1—89—335;
Gawron Advice Letter,
No.
1—89—550.)
Section 308 of your local ordinance would be in conflict with
the Act.
We refer specifically to the language:
“All campaign contributions received by such
persons shall be subject to the provisions of this
Ordinance unless such campaign contributions are used
exclusively for elections held outside the
jurisdiction.”
Under Section 81009.5(b)
of the Act,
any additional reporting
provisions,
such as those included in your ordinance, are
effective if they are limited to elections and ballot measures
which are voted on solely in your jurisdiction.
The wording of
Section 308 inadvertently includes contributions which may be
received specifically for an election outside of your
jurisdiction,
thereby imposing your jurisdiction’s reporting
requirements on campaign activity exclusively controlled by the
Political Reform Act.
We suggest that the wording be changed to
avoid this problem.
3
In Section
303 of your ordinance,the reference to Government
Code Section 82030(b) (9)
may be incorrect as that section exempts
loans from family members from the definition of “income.”
Government Code Section 82028(b) (3)
may be the more accurate’
citation.
If your concern is that a candidate may use unregulated
contributions for elections outside yox jurisdiction for his or
her election in the jurisdiction,
we ndte that Section 85201 of
the Act requires the expenditure of campaign funds for an.election
to come only from a bank account opened in connection with that
election.


We regret that we may not comment further on your city’s
proposed ordinance,
however,
such comment would be beyond our
jurisdictional mandate.
We hope that you find this limited
comment helpful.
Should you have any additional questions,
you
may contact this office at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General
File No.
1—94—111
Page
3
By
Daniel
Counsel, Legal Division
-J

sections.question_synthetic: Does a local campaign reform ordinance comply with the Political Reform Act, specifically regarding campaign contribution reporting requirements and the applicability of local ordinance provisions to contributions received for elections outside the jurisdiction?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Section 308 of the local ordinance conflicts with the Act because it imposes the jurisdiction's reporting requirements on campaign contributions specifically designated for elections outside the jurisdiction; the FPPC recommends rewording to limit application to elections and ballot measures voted on solely within the jurisdiction, and notes a potential citation error in Section 303.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections with headers; extracted from body text. Document is FPPC response to informal advice request dated March 24, 1994. FPPC clarifies its limited jurisdictional scope regarding local ordinance interpretation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does a local campaign reform ordinance comply with the Political Reform Act, specifically regarding campaign contribution reporting requirements and the applicability of local ordinance provisions to contributions received for elections outside the jurisdiction?

CONCLUSION: Section 308 of the local ordinance conflicts with the Act because it imposes the jurisdiction's reporting requirements on campaign contributions specifically designated for elections outside the jurisdiction; the FPPC recommends rewording to limit application to elections and ballot measures voted on solely within the jurisdiction, and notes a potential citation error in Section 303.

Scott Zundel City Attorney 69-825 Highway 111 Rancho Mirage, CA 92270 Re: Your Request For Advice Our File No. 1—94—111 Dear Mr. Zundel: We respond to your March 24, 1994, letter requesting Commission advice regarding your local ordinance on campaign reform. As your letter does not request advice with regard to a specific set of facts, we have treated your request as one for informal advice. 1 Under the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) 2 , the Commission is empowered to give advice with respect to the provisions of the Act. (Section 83114.) While the interpretation of local ordinances regulating campaign finance are generally beyond the scope of Commission advice (g Kunkel Advice Letter, No. 1—89—598), the Act does contain several provisions regarding the implementation of such ordinances. (Sections 81009.5, 81013 and 85100.) The Commission’s advice must necessarily be limited to the application of those sections. (Gawron Advice Letter, No. 1—89—550.) We caution that the Commission cannot provide advice on the operation of your local ordinance. Ketner Advice Letter, No. 1-93—464.) Primary responsibility, for interpreting the ordinance will fall on you, the city attorney. (Kunkel Advice Letter, supra.) Generally, where the provisions of a local law conflict with the Act, the local law is superseded. (Section 81013.) However, the Act does not prevent a local government from imposing lower campaign contribution limits for candidates for elective office in its own jurisdiction. (Section 85101.) Accordingly, the Act does not prevent local jurisdictions from either imposing lower contribution limits or from imposing other political campaign requirements so long as the requirements do not prevent candidates from complying with the Act. (Van Winkle Advice Letter, No. 1—89—335; Gawron Advice Letter, No. 1—89—550.) Section 308 of your local ordinance would be in conflict with the Act. embedding.summary: The FPPC provides limited informal advice to the City Attorney of Rancho Mirage regarding compliance of the city's local campaign reform ordinance with the Political Reform Act, identifying a specific conflict in Section 308 and a potential citation error in Section 303.
